coredata.dc:title: Engineering of 0D/1D architectures in 3D networks over CDs/PPy-CPP biomass foam with high efficiency on seawater evaporation
coredata.dc:creator: Sun, Lei
Zhang, Xiang
Yuan, Hao
Cong, Hongmin
Shi, Yuxing
Lu, Jialin
Shi, Weilong
Guo, Feng
coredata.dc:description: Converting waste biomass into solar evaporator foam has been recognized as an effective strategy for waste recycling and solving the problem of water shortage, but problems of salt deposition and mechanical stability seriously hinder their practical application. Herein, this work proposes a sustainable and high mechanical strength biomass foam (CDs/PPy-CPP) evaporator with zero-dimensional/one-dimensional (0D/1D) hierarchical structure by hydrophilic carbon dots (CDs) and tubular polypyrrole (PPy) are seamlessly integrated into the outer surface of the three-dimensional (3D) carbonized pomelo peel (CPP) biomass foam. The evaporator achieved a high-efficient evaporation rate of up to 2.46 kg m−2 h−1 in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution at 1 sun irradiation, and similarly exhibited outstanding salient evaporation efficiency and resistance to salt deposition in 25 wt% NaCl solution, as well as prominent swage purification ability. Excellent photothermal conversion efficiency is demonstrated attributed to the 0D/1D structure in 3D networks of the CDs/PPy-CPP evaporator, as well as superior mechanical and water transport properties based on the multilayer mesh structure and microscopic water transport pores. The research intends to offer a low-cost, energy-efficient, and environmentally friendly approach to obtain multifunctional photothermal biomass foams and demonstrate the potential applications in seawater desalinization and swage purification.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Seawater desalination
Waste biomass
Anti-salt deposition
Solar interface evaporator
0D/1D structure

xocs:hub-sec.xocs:hub-sec-title: Green and Sustainable Science and Technology

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of CDs and CDs/CPP foam
Water transport and anti-salt deposition performance

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge the funding support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21906072 and 22006057 ), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province ( BK20190982 ), “Doctor of Mass entrepreneurship and innovation” Project in Jiangsu Province.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Synthetic route of CDs/PPy-CPP foam. (b) Process of tubular pyrrole polymerization on CPP foam. (c) Electrochemical etching of graphite rods into CDs solution. (d) Process of loading CDs on PPy-CPP foam. (e) Digital photograph of the CDs/PPy-CPP foam.
SEM images of (a) PP, (b) CPP and (c) CDs/PPy-CPP. (d) High-resolution SEM image of CDs/PPy-CPP. (e, f) TEM and (g) HRTEM and (h) corresponding elemental mapping images of CDs/PPy-CPP foam.

(a) Dynamic contact angle images of CPP, PPy-CPP, CDs/CPP and CDs/PPy-CPP. (b) Vertical transport behavior of water in CPP and CDs/PPy-CPP foams stained with methyl blue dye (10 g/L). (c) Thermal imaging images of heat movement at the bottom of CDs/PPy-CPP foam. (d) Optical images of self-desalting process on the CDs/PPy-CPP foam surface under 1 sun irradiation. (e) Photographs of anti-salt deposition ability for CDs/PPy-CPP foam during solar evaporation process in different concentrations of the NaCl solution (3.5, 7, 15 and 25 wt%). (f) Schematic diagram of water transport and salt diffusion over CDs/PPy-CPP foam. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)

(a) UV–vis-NIR absorption spectra of as-prepared samples. (b) Schematic diagram of photothermal effect analysis unit. (c) Temperature change curves, (d) infrared thermography photos and (e) surface roughness of PP, CPP, CDs/PP, PPy-CPP, CDs/PPy-CPP samples in the dry condition via the laser confocal scanning microscope.

(a) Water weight change versus time for pure water and the prepared sample under 1 sun irradiation. (b) Continuous evaporation efficiency of as-prepared foams under 1 sun irradiation within 3 h. (c) Evaporation rates of CDs/PPy-CPP foam at different light intensities. (d) Evaporation rates of CDs/PPy-CPP foams under 1 sun irradiation with different salt concentrations. (e) 8 cycles of CDs/PPy-CPP evaporator tested under 3.5 wt% NaCl solution under 1 sun irradiation. (f) Comparison of evaporation rates of various reported biomass evaporators under 1 sun irradiation.

(a) Optical images of real solar desalination system from side view and top view. (b) Actual evaporation efficiency of the outdoor test over CDs/PPy-CPP evaporator. (c) Changes in the absorbance of MB and MO in the condensate collected after simulated solar evaporation of dye wastewater in 3.5 % NaCl simulated seawater. (d) The pH change of condensate in acidic (CH3COOH, 0.5 mol/L) and alkaline (NaOH, 0.025 mol/L) solutions in 3.5 % NaCl simulated seawater after evaporation. (e) Water purity test with a multimeter at a fixed inter-electrode distance.

head.ce:title: Engineering of 0D/1D architectures in 3D networks over CDs/PPy-CPP biomass foam with high efficiency on seawater evaporation

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Energy and Power, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Energy and Power, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212003, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Material Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212003, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Material Science and Engineering
Jiangsu University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Material Science and Engineering, Jiangsu University of Science and Technology, Zhenjiang, Jiangsu 212003, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Converting waste biomass into solar evaporator foam has been recognized as an effective strategy for waste recycling and solving the problem of water shortage, but problems of salt deposition and mechanical stability seriously hinder their practical application. Herein, this work proposes a sustainable and high mechanical strength biomass foam (CDs/PPy-CPP) evaporator with zero-dimensional/one-dimensional (0D/1D) hierarchical structure by hydrophilic carbon dots (CDs) and tubular polypyrrole (PPy) are seamlessly integrated into the outer surface of the three-dimensional (3D) carbonized pomelo peel (CPP) biomass foam. The evaporator achieved a high-efficient evaporation rate of up to 2.46 kg m−2 h−1 in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution at 1 sun irradiation, and similarly exhibited outstanding salient evaporation efficiency and resistance to salt deposition in 25 wt% NaCl solution, as well as prominent swage purification ability. Excellent photothermal conversion efficiency is demonstrated attributed to the 0D/1D structure in 3D networks of the CDs/PPy-CPP evaporator, as well as superior mechanical and water transport properties based on the multilayer mesh structure and microscopic water transport pores. The research intends to offer a low-cost, energy-efficient, and environmentally friendly approach to obtain multifunctional photothermal biomass foams and demonstrate the potential applications in seawater desalinization and swage purification.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Water is irreplaceable in social life, and it is a life, resource, and strategic resource. The reserves of global fresh water resources are very limited, and most of the reserves are in polar ice caps and glaciers, and the fresh water resources that can be directly used by human beings only account for 0.00768 % of the global total water volume [1]. Seawater desalination is considered to be one of the most effective methods for alleviating the global shortage of freshwater resources [2]. Traditional seawater desalination techniques include multi-effect distillation, multistage flash distillation, and reverse osmosis [3–7], which require the consumption of large amounts of fossil fuels and need to replace extremely expensive parts. Consequently, the development of low-cost and energy-efficient alternative technologies is important to address the challenges of global freshwater shortages and the energy crisis. Solar-driven interfacial evaporation heats the gas–liquid interface through a local energy conversion [8–10], which is considered an encouraging technique to alleviate the shortage of fresh water that has received increasing research attention due to its sustainable and renewable properties [11,12]. Nevertheless, there are still some unsolved problems, such as high price, low evaporation efficiency and salt accumulation, which affect the performance of the evaporator. There is thereby an urgent need but it is still a significant challenge to develop a high-efficient solar-driven interfacial evaporator with internal multichannels, excellent insulation performance and low cost [13,14].

Large-scale applications of porous three-dimensional (3D) carbon-based materials with micro-nano structures [15], especially carbonized sustainable and renewable biomass materials, have been prepared and applied to solar evaporators, such as carrots [16], potatoes [17], eggplants [18], mushrooms [19], bamboo [20], and straw [21], etc. However, for most of the carbonized biomass, some limitations such as narrow absorption spectral range, poor impact resistance and weak resistance to salt deposition during solar-driven interfacial evaporation, which is fatal to the stable and efficient operation of the evaporator in the long term [22]. Recently, carbon dots (CDs) [23], as an emerging carbon-based nanomaterials, have received immense attention due to outstanding light harvesting performance, low-cost and environmental friendliness [24]. As quasi-spherical carbon nanoparticles, carbon cores are the main component of CDs, accompanied by many hydrophilic functional groups, such as carboxylic acids, epoxy resins, alcohols, carbon groups, and amines [25–27]. Meanwhile, polypyrrole (PPy), as a simply prepared light-absorbing black polymer, is widely used in the field of photothermal conversion due to its full spectrum high light absorption and high photothermal conversion efficiency [28–30]. In addition, PPy contains abundant hydrophilic groups, such as –NH, which can effectively transport water molecules [31]. Hence, combined CDs and PPy with suitable carbonized biomass insulating materials, their components not only possess excellent board-spectrum light absorption and hydrophilicity, but also allow thermal confinement to the interface where photothermal evaporation occurs.

From the structure of the solar evaporator, zero-dimensional (0D)/one-dimensional (1D) materials have attracted much attention due to their interesting geometrical and particular chemical properties [32], which can be summarized as follows: (i) 1D materials have a long axis for efficient absorption of incident sunlight and short path lengths for the transport of water molecules [31,33,34]; (ii) similar to biomimetic veins, 0D nanomaterials could provide more adsorption sites, further enhancing the transport of water molecules of the 1D substrate materials [26,35,36]. Thus, the zero-dimensional/one-dimensional (0D/1D) structure formed by loading CDs on the tubular PPy was utilized to combine with the 3D crosslinked network structure of carbonized biomass material to form a multilayered structure, which not only facilitates multiple reflections and scattering of light in the interior, but also further accelerates the transport of water during evaporation, improving the anti-salt deposition performance of the evaporator [37].

In this study, we designed a sustainable high mechanical strength biomass solar evaporator with cross-linking network structure by carbonized pomelo peels (CPP) and adding tubular PPy and CDs. In particular, benefiting from the 0D/1D structures of CDs/PPy in 3D CPP networks, the CDs/PPy-CPP composite biomass evaporator exhibited a superior water evaporation performance with an evaporation rate of 2.46 kg m−2 h−1 in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution at 1 sunlight intensity, and a solar vapor conversion efficiency of up to 157.45 %. In addition, based on the remarkable mechanical and water transport properties of the multilayer lattice structure and microscopic water transport holes, the evaporation efficiency of the CDs/PPy-CPP biomass evaporator in 25 wt% NaCl solution is still outstanding and exhibits excellent resistance to salt deposition. More importantly, the CDs/PPy-CPP evaporator can also provide a biocompatible platform for achieving solution purification of strong acids/bases and dyes as well as obtaining high-quality condensate from wastewater.

The fresh grapefruit used in this work was sourced from the local market in Zhenjiang, Jiangsu Province, China. The yellow skin of the grapefruit was removed and the skin was then freeze-dried to form biomass foam precursors. Pyrrole (C4H5N), iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), methyl orange (MO, C14H14N3SO3Na), ethanol (C2H6O) and sodium chloride (NaCl) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., ltd. Expandable polyethylene (EPE) sponge was purchased from online Taobao website.

The fresh pomelo peel was pretreated and freeze-dried for 30 h. Then freeze-dried precursor was put into a tube furnace for heat treatment, and the parameters are set as: 260 °C, 5 °C/min, 30 min, pass N2. On this process, carbonized pomelo peel (CPP) biomass foam was obtained.

The deposition of polypyrrole (PPy) on the CPP biomass foam to form PPy-CPP biomass foam was prepared by chemical oxidative polymerization [31]. MO (0.185 g) was first dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water solution and sonicated for 10 min until completely dissolved after which pyrrole (Py) solution (0.453 g) was added, then CPP was immersed in the mixed solution and sonicated for 2 h. FeCl3·6H2O (1.827 g) was dissolved in 40 mL of deionized water, then quickly added to the Py/CPP biomass foam-soaked solution, sonicated and mixed evenly. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a refrigerator at −5 °C and left for 24 h. After that, the mixture was taken out of the beaker with tweezers and placed in an empty beaker. Then, 20 mL of ethanol and deionized water were poured alternately and washed four times and dried to obtain PPy-CPP biomass foam.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CDs and CDs/CPP foam
ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) solid powder are prepared in the laboratory by the electrochemical method based on our previous research [38,39]. Two graphite rods were inserted into ultrapure water as anode and cathode, and a bias voltage of 30 V was applied between the two electrodes using a direct current power supply, which was followed by continuous stirring conditions for 10 days. Then, the dark black solution was formed in the reaction cell and the dark solution was filtered twice and freeze-dried as powder.

For the synthesis of CDs/CPP foam, 0.02 g of the above prepared CDs powder was dispersed in 5 mL of anhydrous ethanol and sonicated for 15 min at room temperature, and the CPP biomass foam was first immersed in a beaker containing 10 mL of deionized water for 5 min to prevent downward diffusion of the CDs ethanol solution. Next, the CPP biomass foam was taken out with forceps and put in an empty glass dish, and the CDs solution was evenly dropped onto the upper surface of CPP several times with a dropper. After the drops, the samples were placed in an oven and heated at 60 °C for 6 h. Finally, the CDs/CPP biomass foam was obtained.

0.02 g of the prepared CDs powder was dispersed in 5 mL of anhydrous ethanol and sonicated for 15 min at room temperature. Then, the PPy-CPP biomass foam was first immersed in a beaker containing 10 mL of deionized water for 5 min to prevent downward diffusion of the CDs ethanol solution. Next, the PPy-CPP biomass foam was taken out with forceps and put in an empty glass dish, and the CDs solution was evenly dropped onto the upper surface of PPy-CPP several times with a dropper. After the drops, the samples were placed in an oven and heated at 60 °C for 6 h. In this process, CDs/PPy-CPP biomass foam was obtained.

Sunlight seawater evaporation experiments of as-prepared samples were carried out at a relative humidity of 42 ± 5 % using a solar simulator (PLS-SXE300+/300UV, Perfect Light) with a solar density of 100 mW cm−2 (1 sun). The NaCl solution with mass fraction of 3.5 wt% was used to simulate the seawater. The sample floated on the surface of a beaker containing saline, supported by an EPE sponge 3 mm thick (5 mm middle radius). During the evaporation process of water, the change in water mass was monitored in-real time using a high precision electronic balance (accuracy 0.1 mg) which was connected with the computer.
Corresponding material characterization, testing instrumentation and calculation of solar vapor energy efficiency are provided in the Supporting Information.

The synthetic route of the CDs/PPy-CPP foam is given in Fig. 1 a. Firstly, the raw PP was vacuum-dried with a freeze dryer, removed water from the biomass without destroying the fluffy structure, and then heated and carbonized in a tubular furnace to obtain excellent mechanical properties. PPy nanotubes were deposited in situ on the CPP biomass foam using MO as a simple template and subjected to chemical oxidation (Fig. 1b). MO has hydrophilic ionic groups and hydrophobic parts of aromatic systems, which can self-assemble through hydrophobic interactions to form micelle-like aggregates. The liquid–solid interface formed during this process is the template for the growth of PPy nanotubes, which are generated from zwitterionic molecules connected by dipole–dipole coupling and further stabilized by π-π stacking [40,41]. CDs solutions were prepared according to a typical electrochemical method (Fig. 1c), the graphite rods were made to form a deep black solution in the reaction cell by applying a DC power supply. Then, the solution was filtered and lyophilized to obtain CDs solid powder. Furthermore, CDs were dispersed in ethanol solution for dropwise addition into PPy-CPP foam (Fig. 1d). Due to the surface tension difference between water and ethanol, the water adsorbed in the foam retards the downward diffusion of CDs, thus confining the CDs to the surface layer of the foam. A digital photograph of a regular shape and black appearance of the CDs/PPy-CPP foam is shown in Fig. 1e, which is favorable to light absorption, and the 3D porous structure is suitable for interfacial water evaporation.

The microscopic morphology and composition of biomass foams were investigated by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and energy dispersive X-ray analysis (EDS). As given in Fig. 2 a, PP is a typical biomass foam with a network-like pore structure, and after heating treated (260 °C), the pore structure of CPP is still retained (Fig. 2b). Through mechanical property tests, PP has certain mechanical properties (Fig. S1a), and CPP still maintains relatively excellent mechanical properties after heat treatment (Fig. S1b), giving the CDs/PPy-CPP evaporator with excellent mechanical properties with 13 N maximum tensile force. A visual comparison of the mechanical properties of PP, eggplant and carrot revealed that both eggplant and carrot showed weaker structures before and after heat treatment, while CPP directly exhibited better mechanical properties (Fig. S1c), Further exploring the reason for the better mechanical properties of CPP, speculating that probably after carbonization, the retained network-like structure enables CPP to have better resistance to impact (Fig. S1d). From the SEM images in the side direction, the multilayer stacked structure (Fig.S2) and the specific pores (Fig.S3) can be also observed. Fig. 2c illustrates that the loading of CDs and tubular PPy on the CPP biomass foam does not destroy the above structure. By further amplification, SEM in Fig. 2d and TEM in Fig. 2e indicate the distribution of tubular PPy on the surface of CPP. Moreover, as provided in Fig. 2f, the CDs are uniformly distributed on the surface of the tubular PPy. Further observation by high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM), Fig. 2g displays a significant lattice spacing of 0.21 nm for CDs [42], which is consistent with the CDs prepared by electrochemical method (Fig.S4) [38], and it is tentatively speculated that CDs are successfully incorporated in the PPy-CPP foam to form CDs/PPy-CPP composite system with the unique 0D/1D structure in 3D network. Fig. 2h presents high-angle annular dark-field scanning transmission electron microscopy (HAADF-SEM) and elemental mapping images of CDs/PPy-CPP, and the available sample contain C, N, and O elements with uniform distribution. In addition, the presence of C, N, and O elements can also be demonstrated based on the elemental content obtained by EDS spectrum in Fig.S5. In the Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) spectrum of Fig.S6, all samples exhibit the similar peaks at the same position. The peak at 3500 cm−1 is ascribed to the hydroxyl functional group, and the peak at 3100 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibration of the CH bonds [31]. Besides, the peak at 1620 cm−1 is attributed to the stretching vibration of the CO bonds, and the peak at 1405 cm−1 corresponds to the asymmetric stretching vibration of the C-bond, and the absorption peak at 1050 cm−1 is corresponded to the planar deformation vibration of the CH bond. Notably, PPy-CPP and CDs/PPy-CPP samples displayed new peaks at 3400 and 780 cm−1, matching the characteristic stretching vibration peaks of NH bonds of PPy, respectively [31,43]. According to the Raman spectra in Fig.S7, it can be concluded that the d-band (graphitic carbon structure of sp2 hybridized carbon atoms) and G-band (disordered carbon atom structure) of CPP sample are found at 1550 and 1431 cm−1, which are caused by the low temperature carbonization treatment of PP foam [31]. Compared with CPP, a bit of blue shift in both D and G band peaks can be obtained for the CDs/CPP, PPy-CPP, CDs/PPy-CPP samples, which also confirmed the interaction among CDs, PPy and CPP.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Water transport and anti-salt deposition performance
ce:section.ce:para: Important factors for the continuous operation of solar evaporators include strong water transmission capacity and excellent resistance to salt deposition. For the first aspect, a strong water transport capacity can provide sufficient water for the solar thermal interface. In general, hydrophilic three-dimensional (3D) porous materials have good water retention and transport capacity. In Fig. 3 a, all the samples were hydrophilic, but only CPP required the longest permeation time of 6.0 s during this experimental procedure, while the other samples required only 600 ms. This is due to the fact that pomelo peel produces a large amount of tar content during low temperature carbonization, which can easily block the pores, causing water obstruction in its internal diffusion. In contrast, the rapid penetrations of the composite foams were enhanced after loading CDs and PPy, which is ascribed to the introduction of hydrophilic groups, such as OH and NH groups in the evaporator framework, effectively enhancing the interaction between water molecules and the evaporator [25,26,31]. N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms and pore size distributions were measured and provided in Fig.S8. The specific surface area based on the Brunauer Emmett Teller (SBET) of CPP, CDs/CPP, PPy-CPP and CDs/CPP-CPP samples were calculated to be 2.6, 3.4, 2.5 and 3.4 m2/g, which suggested that no significant change in the specific surface area occurred. The average pore size distribution of the foam carbon is given in Table S1. Similarly, the dye methyl blue is used to verify the water transport capability of the evaporator more intuitively. In Fig. 3b, it can be seen that the blue dye solution was rapidly stained with the white paper spread on the top of the foams (35 mm high), but the immersion time varies between CPP (120 s) and CDs/PPy-CPP (20 s), which also indicates from the side that the water transport performance of the CDs/PPy-CPP foam with unique 0D/1D architectures in 3D networks has been greatly improved. According to Fig. 3c, the surface temperature change of the CDs/PPy-CPP foam was observed with an infrared camera, and it found that the surface temperature of the foam was gradually increased from bottom to top as the hot water was transported from the bottom to the top. Excellent resistance to salt deposition is the basis for continuous and stable operation of the evaporator. For the self-desalination performance test in Fig. 3d, the evaporator was floated in the simulated seawater, and 2 g NaCl was placed on CDs/PPy-CPP foam and then irradiated under one sun. After 70 min of light irradiation, the salt particles on the surface of CDs/PPy-CPP foam were gradually dissolved, which is attributed to the following reasons: (i) the rich pore structure and impressed water wettability of CPP provide a prerequisite for the self-desalination properties of continuous capillary pumping [44–46]; (ii) the surface temperature of the exposed foam increases and its surface tension changes with temperature, resulting in a thermal capillary effect; (iii) when the surface tension is driven by the concentration, the surface salt concentration of CDs/PPy-CPP foam is saturated and the surface energy is high, resulting in the solute capillary effect; (iv) the diffusion effect caused by the difference in salt concentration between the evaporator surface and the simulated seawater also contributes to the transport of surface salts into the simulated seawater [31,47–50]. Furthermore, the CDs/PPy-CPP evaporator foam supported by EPE foam was tested for desalination in simulated salt water at different salt concentrations of 3.5 wt%, 7 wt%, 15 wt%, and 25 wt%, respectively. In Fig. 3e, after 5 h of continuous run, no deposited salt particles were found in 3.5 wt%, 7 wt% and 15 wt% saline. Nevertheless, in the case of 25 wt% saline, some salt particles were deposited on the evaporator surface. By observing the evaporator surface (Fig.S9), it is found that the surface density of the area that produces salt deposition is higher than that of other areas, which blocks the pore channel and cannot quickly transports water to the upper surface, leading to the deterioration of salt particle deposition. In spite of this, the CDs/PPy-CPP evaporator foam still has excellent salt deposition resistance. After a desalting evaporator as shown in Fig. 3f, its rich internal pore structure improves the efficiency of seawater transfer and resolves the salt particles on the surface.

Optical absorption characteristics are quite important for photothermal materials [51,52], so the light absorption properties of as-prepared foams were analyzed by UV–vis-NIR absorption spectroscopy. In Fig. 4 a, it is clearly visible that PP exhibits the limited solar absorption with the maximum absorption edge of 500 nm, while after low temperature heat treatment, CPP has a black surface and the absorption edge has significant redshift, which has better light absorption performance. Moreover, after loading with PPy or CDs, the light absorption capacities of the CPP were further enhanced. Notably, when CDs were loaded on PPy-CPP foam, an optimal light absorption was achieved in the whole wavelength range (300–1500 nm), which may be due to the synergistic effect of CDs and PPy with 0D/1D structure of CDs/PPy-CPP foam further allowing the incoming electric field to strongly penetrate the contracted geometry effect and contribute to broadband absorption [53–56]. Next, to explore the photothermal conversion properties of as-prepared foams, the surface temperature changes of the prepared samples were further tested under simulated light irradiation using the infrared camera (Fig. 4b). From the Fig. 4c and d, after four repetitions for each time, the surface temperatures of all the samples could reach the maximum value and can be visualized in the following order: CDs/PPy-CPP (73.1 ℃) > PPy-CPP (66.5 ℃) > CDs/PPy (65.6 ℃) > CPP (61.5 ℃) > PP (41.1 ℃), which means that the assembly of CDs and PPy with 0D/1D structure on the 3D CPP network not only broadens the light absorption range but also promotes non-radiative compounding that always releases thermal energy. Similarly, in contrast to drying conditions, the CDs/PPy-CPP foam still maintains good photothermal conversion characteristics under wet conditions of real immersion in simulated seawater (Fig.S10). To confirm the important role of the structure in the photothermal materials, the surface roughness of as-prepared foams was tested by the laser confocal scanning microscope (Fig. 4e) and corresponding values of Ra (arithmetical mean roughness value, Unit: μm) and Rz (maximum height of roughness profile, Unit: μm) are listed in Table S2. After heat treatment of PP, the surface roughness of the obtained CPP foam increased significantly, caused by the shrinkage of the skeleton stacking of the carbonized teak peel. And after loading CDs and PPy on the CPP foam, due to the 0D/1D structure in 3D network of CDs/PPy-CPP foam, the surface roughness was further enhanced by the formation of irregular protrusions and micro-nano-pore structure on the evaporator surface, as well as the increase of the effective area of the light absorption interface.

High evaporation efficiency is crucial for evaporator performance, thus real-time monitoring of CDs/PPy-CPP evaporator foam under 100 mW cm−2 solar radiation, and systematic evaluation of solar steam production capacity of CDs/PPy-CPP evaporator. In Fig. 5 a, excluding the natural evaporation of water (0.156 kg m−2 in 1 h), this test discovered that both CDs/CPP and PPy-CPP evaporators exhibited superior evaporation efficiencies compared to CPP due to the excellent photothermal conversion of CDs and PPy, and it is worth noting that the CDs/PPy-CPP evaporator loaded with both CDs and PPy exhibited best evaporation efficiency. In the absence of foam loading, CDs and PPy can only be dispersed in solution for water evaporation, not only inefficient but also difficult to recover (Fig.S11).To further verify the evaporation efficiency of the synthesized sponges, the simulation tests were extended for 3 h (Fig. 5b), and the order of magnitude of the evaporation efficiencies remained as follows: CDs/PPy-CPP (2.46 kg m−2 h−1) > PPy-CPP (1.38 kg m−2 h−1) > CDs/CPP (1.24 kg m−2 h−1) > CPP (0.36 kg m−2 h−1). To investigate whether the as-prepared CDs/PPy-CPP evaporator can still maintain excellent evaporation efficiency at different light intensities or different salt concentrations, which reflects the perfect environmental adaptability of the evaporator. In Fig. 5c, a test of the evaporation rate of CDs/PPy-CPP foams was carried out at different light intensities, and the evaporation efficiency was found to increase proportionally with the influence of light intensity on the surface temperature of the evaporator. Next, different evaporation efficiencies were tested at different salt concentrations (3.5 wt%, 7 wt% and 15 wt%). In Fig. 5d, the evaporation efficiencies of the CDs/PPy-CPP evaporator were unaffected and no salt accumulation was observed on its surface, which was attributed to the excellent water transport performance of the evaporator, demonstrating that the evaporator maintains a high evaporation efficiency even under high salinity conditions. However, the efficiency is slightly reduced in 25 wt% concentration because of the production of some salt particles on the CDs/PPy-CPP evaporator surface. The stability of the evaporation efficiency of the evaporator is also a challenge when performing seawater desalination. The CDs/PPy-CPP foam was then cyclically tested and the evaporation efficiency was discovered to remain around 2.3 kg m−2 h−1 for each time (Fig. 5e), indicating that the CDs/PPy-CPP evaporator possesses an outstanding stability of evaporation efficiency. Then, to highlight the performance advantages of the prepared biomass evaporator, the CDs/PPy-CPP evaporator was compared with other previously reported biomass evaporators (Fig. 5f and Table S3), and the CDs/PPy-CPP evaporator in this work exhibits a better evaporation efficiency than most current biomass evaporators.

To demonstrate the possibility of actual freshwater production by CDs/PPy-CPP evaporator, a simple steam water collection device was designed and the evaporation unit placed on the open roof of the experimental building. The condensate was collected through a glass cover and recorded the solar intensity and surface temperature of the samples of that day (June 28, 2023, Zhenjiang, China).

In Fig. 6 a, through the transpiration of the synthesized CDs/PPy-CPP foam on the water under the solar light irradiation, it can be clearly seen that on the top of the container is filled with distilled water droplets. Meanwhile, with the change of irradiation time, from 8:00 to 12:00, it can see that the evaporation efficiency gradually increases (Fig. 6b), which is consistent with above results that the light intensity increases and the evaporation efficiency increases (Fig. 5c). Stable water evaporation under organic dyes or under acidic and alkaline conditions, as well as obtaining condensate that meets domestic water standards, are also essential to the evaporator's ability to stabilize evaporation and water purification. UV–vis absorption spectroscopy measurements were carried out on the simulated organic dye wastewater and the collected condensate (Fig. S12), and it was observed that no relevant contaminants were found to be present in the collected water, demonstrating that the CDs/PPy-CPP evaporator foams have a preeminent removal performance for the organic dyes (Fig. 6c). Then, the CDs/PPy-CPP evaporator foam was applied to acidic (pH = 2.5) and alkaline (pH = 12.7) solutions for solar-to-desalination experiments, and the pH of both condensates form acidic and alkaline environment displayed the neutral results, confirming that the surface CDs/PPy-CPP evaporator foam possesses splendid resistance to acids and alkalines. (Fig. 6d). Moreover, the seawater ion separation performance of the foam was tested and the quality of purified water was evaluated with its resistance value [57]. The ohmic values of different water samples were measured with a multimeter without changing the electrode spacing (Fig. 6e), and the results presented as follows: 25 wt% NaCl solution (58.85 KΩ) < domestic water (Zhenjiang, China) (95.49 KΩ) < condensated water (126.22 KΩ) < deionized water (137.42 KΩ), indicating that the quality of the condensated water is very closely matched to deionized water and meets the standards for domestic water.

In summary, a sustainable and high mechanical strength biomass solar evaporator was designed by carbonizing waste biomass pomelo peel and loading tubular polypyrrole and carbon dots on its biomass skeleton with cross-linked network structure. The CDs/PPy-CPP biomass evaporator reached an evaporation rate of 2.46 kg m−2 h−1 in 3.5 wt% NaCl solution under 1 light intensity, and also remained prominent evaporation efficiency in 25 wt% NaCl solution, as well as demonstrated excellent resistance to salt deposition with no salt deposition on the surface over the course of long-term experiments. The CDs/PPy-CPP evaporator can also offer a biocompatible platform for achieving solution purification of strong acids/bases and dyes, and obtaining high quality condensate from swage purification. Due to the 0D/1D structure in 3D networks of the CDs/PPy-CPP evaporator, it exhibits superior photothermal properties as well as significant mechanical and water transport properties based on the multilayer grid structure and microscopic water transport pores. Considering the low cost of waste biomass materials and the scalability of the manufacturing process, this research provides a reference for further strengthening the use of biomass derivative materials for the preparation of solar seawater desalination and swage purification devices.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the funding support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21906072 and 22006057), the Natural Science Foundation of Jiangsu Province (BK20190982), “Doctor of Mass entrepreneurship and innovation” Project in Jiangsu Province.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cej.2023.147279.

ce:section.ce:para:
